Introduction¶

This is a data analysis practical exercise designed to gain insights into Netflix library offering and strategy and how it has evolved over the years. The data for this analysis was taken from the Netflix Movies and TV Shows sample data set posted by Shivam Bansal on Kaggle. A link to the data set download page can be found here.

In this analysis notebook, I work through loading and exploring the data, cleaning issues found within the data, conduct a category analysis, and supplement the analysis by comparing the data to Netflix stock performance during the same time period.

Loading, Exploring, and Cleaning Data:¶

Loading¶

In [730]:
#import data and pandas
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("netflix_titles.csv")

Cleaning Rating Column¶
In [737]:
#isolate problem rows
rating_valid =[
    "TV-MA", "TV-14", "TV-PG", "R","PG-13", "TV-Y7", "TV-Y","PG","TV-G", "NR", "G", "TV-Y7-FV", "NC-17", "UR", None
]

df["rating_valid"] = df["rating"].isin(rating_valid)

df[df["rating_valid"] == False]
Out[737]:
show_id type title director cast country date_added release_year rating duration listed_in description rating_valid
5541 s5542 Movie Louis C.K. 2017 Louis C.K. Louis C.K. United States April 4, 2017 2017 74 min NaN Movies Louis C.K. muses on religion, eternal love, gi... False
5794 s5795 Movie Louis C.K.: Hilarious Louis C.K. Louis C.K. United States September 16, 2016 2010 84 min NaN Movies Emmy-winning comedy writer Louis C.K. brings h... False
5813 s5814 Movie Louis C.K.: Live at the Comedy Store Louis C.K. Louis C.K. United States August 15, 2016 2015 66 min NaN Movies The comic puts his trademark hilarious/thought... False
In [738]:
#move duration value out of rating column into duration column
df.loc[~df["rating_valid"],"duration"] = df["rating"]
df.loc[~df["rating_valid"], "rating"] = None

df[df["rating_valid"] == False]
Out[738]:
show_id type title director cast country date_added release_year rating duration listed_in description rating_valid

Duration¶
In [739]:
#duration is either minutes or number of seasons
#verify no other data
#verify TV Show type = Seasons and Movie Type = min

mask_type_movie = df["type"] == "TV Show"

mask_duration_isnot_mins = ~df["duration"].str.contains("Season")

df[mask_type_movie & mask_duration_isnot_mins]
Out[739]:
show_id type title director cast country date_added release_year rating duration listed_in description rating_valid
Date Added¶
In [740]:
#verify date added column
#create new column for date added that converts date from string to date as readable by pandas
#also handle errors
df["date_added_parsed"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date_added"].str.strip(), errors = "coerce")

#counts entries that are not dates
df["date_added_parsed"].isna().sum()
df[df["date_added_parsed"].isna()]

#this will tell us what is actually in the date added cells for the NaN rows:
bad = df[df["date_added_parsed"].isna()][["date_added"]]
bad["date_added"].apply(lambda x: repr(x))

#looks like these are not string values, but actual nan values so these are valid we can move on

Release Year¶
In [742]:
#release year convert all entries to numbers
df["release_year_clean"] = pd.to_numeric(df["release_year"], errors="coerce")
#confirm all entries are numaric (ie not "two thousand")
non_numeric = df[df["release_year_clean"].isna()][["title", "release_year"]]

#no issues found
In [743]:
#confirm all entries are 4 digit years and look for years that don't make sense (before the invention of film or in the future):
#using 2021 as top end as that is the last year the data set was updated. 
bad_release_years = df[(df["release_year_clean"] < 1900) | (df["release_year_clean"] > 2021)][["title","release_year"]] 

bad_release_years

#no issues found
Out[743]:
title release_year
Ratings¶

As there were several different rating schemes, particularly in TV, we will group these by Children, Young Adult, and Adult for easier analysis.

In [744]:
#ratings
#group ratings into 3 categories: "children", "young_adult", "adult"
# Define rating groups

children_ratings = ["G", "PG", "TV-Y", "TV-Y7", "TV-Y7-FV", "TV-G"]
young_adult_ratings = ["PG-13", "TV-PG", "TV-14"]
adult_ratings = ["R", "NC-17", "TV-MA", "UR", "NR"]

# Function to map rating to category
def map_rating_category(r):
    if r in children_ratings:
        return "children"
    elif r in young_adult_ratings:
        return "young_adult"
    elif r in adult_ratings:
        return "adult"
    else:
        return "not_listed"  # catches NaN or anything unexpected

# Apply the mapping
df["rating_category"] = df["rating"].apply(map_rating_category)
df["rating_category"].value_counts(dropna = False)
Out[744]:
rating_category
adult          4092
young_adult    3513
children       1195
not_listed        7
Name: count, dtype: int64

Analysis¶

Count by Type¶

In [745]:
#count by type
df["type"].value_counts()
Out[745]:
type
Movie      6131
TV Show    2676
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [746]:
#count by type in table:
type_counts = df["type"].value_counts().reset_index()
type_counts.columns = ["type", "count"]
type_counts
Out[746]:
type count
0 Movie 6131
1 TV Show 2676
Offering Mix¶

Our first observation is that Nextflix offering mix is nearly 3x movies than TV shows. It should be noted however that the data set is missing date removed, so the actual ratio is an unknown moving target. At best we can glean from this over the years covered, Netflix has prioritized acquiring movie titles over TV shows. We will see in later analysis why this is and how their strategy has evolved.

In [747]:
#Bar Chart:
df["type"].value_counts().plot(kind="bar", title="Movies vs TV Shows")
Out[747]:
<Axes: title={'center': 'Movies vs TV Shows'}, xlabel='type'>
In [748]:
#pie chart
df["type"].value_counts().plot(kind="pie", autopct="%1.1f%%")
Out[748]:
<Axes: >

Title Acquisition by year¶

Netflix started acquring Titles very slowly at first then grew rapidly around 2015. This growth aligns with several initiatives by the company including Netflix World Wide, where Netflix became available in nearly every country on earth, and the beginning of Netflix Originals, where netflix became producing their own content in house. We still see that the number of movies acquired year to year, vastly outpaced TV-Shows.

In [749]:
#count type by year:
df["year_added"] = df["date_added_parsed"].dt.year

counts_by_year = (
    df.groupby(["year_added", "type"])
      .size()
      .reset_index(name="count")
)
In [750]:
#pivot data for year, Move, TV show Column
pivot_type_year = counts_by_year.pivot(
    index="year_added",
    columns="type",
    values="count"
)
In [751]:
ax = pivot_type_year.plot.line()
ax.set_xlabel("Year Added")
ax.set_ylabel("Titles Added Each Year")
ax.set_title("Netflix Content Added by Type Over Time")
Out[751]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Netflix Content Added by Type Over Time')
Average Age When Acquired¶

Looking at the average age of the titles acquired each year will also give us insight into Netflix strategy.

In [752]:
#let's find the average age of a movie when it was added then plot the average of each year
df["age_added"] = df["year_added"] - df["release_year"]

#average age by type
avg_age_by_type = (
    df.groupby(["year_added", "type"])["age_added"].mean()
    .reset_index(name = "avg_age")
)
In [753]:
#now pivot table this
pivot_avg_age = avg_age_by_type.pivot(
    index="year_added",
    columns="type",
    values="avg_age"
)
Average Age of Title Analysis¶

Netflix's early offerings were much older titles that they likely were able to purchase at a cheaper rate than newer offerings. This strategy is seen in 2010 and 2011 with movies and again in 2014 with TV shows. Netflix likely went to newer Movie titles following 2011 to appeal to customers looking for recent Movies, and again we see the same behavior in 2015 for TV shows. Unlike movies however, TV show average age continue to decline after 2016 with the rise of Netflix Originals, which were heavily TV show biased. Movies begin trending older again in 2016, but this can be explained by Netflix new global market, which would drive them to add a backlog of titles developed internationally. We will investigate the dynamics of international offerings next.

In [754]:
ax = pivot_avg_age.plot.line()
ax.set_xlabel("Year Added")
ax.set_ylabel("Avg Age of Titles Added")
ax.set_title("Netflix Content Average Age on Year Added")
Out[754]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Netflix Content Average Age on Year Added')

#next let's look at average age by per year by country 
avg_age_by_country = (
    df.groupby(["year_added", "country"])["age_added"].mean()
    .reset_index(name = "avg_age")
)

pivot_avg_age_country = avg_age_by_country.pivot(
    index="year_added",
    columns="country",
    values="avg_age"
)

pivot_avg_age_country

Quick pause to clean country column¶
In [756]:
#updating country column so if there are multiple counties they are split into separate rows
df["country"] = df["country"].str.split(", ")
df_exploded = df.explode("country")

top_countries = (
    df_exploded["country"]
    .value_counts()
    .head(10)
    .index
)

df_top = df_exploded[df_exploded["country"].isin(top_countries)]
In [757]:
#trying again
#next let's look at average age by per year by country 
avg_age_country = (
    df_top.groupby(["year_added", "country", "type"])["age_added"]
    .mean()
    .reset_index()
)
In [758]:
#movie pivot
movies_only = avg_age_country[avg_age_country["type"] == "Movie"]

pivot_movies = movies_only.pivot(
    index="year_added",
    columns="country",
    values="age_added"
)
In [759]:
ax = pivot_movies.plot.line()
ax.set_xlabel("Year Added")
ax.set_ylabel("Avg Age of Titles Added")
ax.set_title("Netflix Content Added by Average Age and Country - Movies")
Out[759]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Netflix Content Added by Average Age and Country - Movies')
In [760]:
#TV Show pivot
TV_Only = avg_age_country[avg_age_country["type"] == "TV Show"]

pivot_TV = TV_Only.pivot(
    index="year_added",
    columns="country",
    values="age_added"
)

ax = pivot_TV.plot.line()
ax.set_xlabel("Year Added")
ax.set_ylabel("Avg Age of Titles Added")
ax.set_title("Netflix Content Added by Average Age and Country - TV")
Out[760]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Netflix Content Added by Average Age and Country - TV')
In [761]:
#count country by year: 
df["year_added"] = df["date_added_parsed"].dt.year

counts_by_country = ( df_top.groupby(["year_added", "country"]) .size() .reset_index(name="count") )

pivot_country_count = counts_by_country.pivot( index="year_added", columns="country", values="count" )

ax = pivot_country_count.plot.line()
ax.set_xlabel("Year Added")
ax.set_ylabel("Number of Titles Added")
ax.set_title("Netflix Content Added by Country")
Out[761]:
Text(0.5, 1.0, 'Netflix Content Added by Country')

Country Data¶

Looking at Movies and TV shows average age at the time of being added by country, we see a similar strategy used for American audience in years prior to what was employed with Netflix Worldwide, where an initial acquisition of older titles was prioritized first, then newer titles were brought in later. This effect is much less pronounced however, suggesting a refinement in Netflix's strategy.

The far more telling info about international titles is the final graph showing the number of movies and shows by country added each year. Again we see the biggest increases in the years immediately after Netflix Worldwide in 2016, but the interesting take away is which countrie saw the largest lift; namely India and the UK. I believe India was heavily selected due to already having a large film industry and it also represents the largest population country Netflix was offered in at the time. (Netflix remains unavailable in China). Similarly an increase in UK offerings is due to an already established film industry combined with a common language with American audiences.

Again, with all of these assertions, one key piece of data missing is date removed. Adding a date removed column would give us better insight to how Netflix is shaping their offering mix. This would be a key insight to their overall strategy.

In [762]:
#graph rating category added by year
counts_by_rating = (
    df.groupby(["year_added", "rating_category"])
      .size()
      .reset_index(name="count")
)
pivot_rating = counts_by_rating.pivot(
    index="year_added",
    columns="rating_category",
    values="count"
)

pivot_rating.plot.line()
Out[762]:
<Axes: xlabel='year_added'>

Rating Analysis¶

Due to the varying methods that are used to rate content, especially TV Shows, I have grouped the ratings into Children, young adult, and adult. We can see from the graph that Netflix maintained a pretty even distribution until 2015 where adult and young adult content outpaced children's content by a wide margin. Ultimately, adult content continues to be the main addition to Netflix catalogue, but adult content and young adult content started declining in 2019, while children's content continued growing until the COVID-19 pandemic. Netflix would have continued adding more children's content during this time to stay competitive with Disney+ which was launched in 2019.

Stock¶

These final two graphs show how the yearly average stock price moved with Netflix's content acquisition. We see there are modest gains after adding older films to build up the overall library in 2011 and again in 2014 with TV shows, but the real growth in stock happened as the largest increase to their offering happened as they went international starting in 2016. The investment in thousands of new titles, from international markets paid off as stock price increased.

In [763]:
#adding stock price info
df_stock = pd.read_csv("NetflixStockPrice.txt")
df_stock.head()

In [765]:
fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

# Left axis: content counts
ax1.plot(merged["year"], merged["Movie"], label="Movies Added", color="blue")
ax1.plot(merged["year"], merged["TV Show"], label="TV Shows Added", color="green")
ax1.set_xlabel("Year")
ax1.set_ylabel("Content Added")
ax1.tick_params(axis="y")

# Right axis: stock price
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(merged["year"], merged["average_stock_price"], label="Average Stock Price", color="red")
ax2.set_ylabel("Average Stock Price")
ax2.tick_params(axis="y", labelcolor="red")

# Combined legend
lines_1, labels_1 = ax1.get_legend_handles_labels()
lines_2, labels_2 = ax2.get_legend_handles_labels()
ax1.legend(lines_1 + lines_2, labels_1 + labels_2, loc="upper left")

plt.title("Movies Added, TV Shows Added, and Netflix Stock Price by Year")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
In [766]:
merged = pivot_avg_age.merge(
    df_stock,
    left_index=True,
    right_on="year"
)

fig, ax1 = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 6))

# Left axis: content counts
ax1.plot(merged["year"], merged["Movie"], label="Movies Age", color="blue")
ax1.plot(merged["year"], merged["TV Show"], label="TV Shows Age", color="green")
ax1.set_xlabel("Year")
ax1.set_ylabel("Content Average Age")
ax1.tick_params(axis="y")

# Right axis: stock price
ax2 = ax1.twinx()
ax2.plot(merged["year"], merged["average_stock_price"], label="Average Stock Price", color="red")
ax2.set_ylabel("Average Stock Price")
ax2.tick_params(axis="y", labelcolor="red")

# Combined legend
lines_1, labels_1 = ax1.get_legend_handles_labels()
lines_2, labels_2 = ax2.get_legend_handles_labels()
ax1.legend(lines_1 + lines_2, labels_1 + labels_2, loc="upper left")

plt.title("Average Age of Content Added, and Netflix Stock Price by Year")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

Key Findings¶

  1. Netflix’s early catalog strategy relied heavily on older, likely inexpensive films Across 2008–2012, the average age of movies added was often 10–20+ years old, reflecting a cost‑efficient approach to building a library quickly. This allowed Netflix to enter streaming with breadth before securing rights to newer titles.
  2. TV shows initially lagged behind movies but became strategically central after 2016 TV additions were minimal in the early years, but the average age of TV content dropped sharply after 2016, coinciding with the rise of Netflix Originals, which are typically added the same year they’re produced.
  3. A major inflection point occurred in 2015–2016 We see a dramatic surge in both movies and TV shows added during this period. This aligns with:
    • Netflix’s global expansion into 130+ countries
    • The acceleration of original content production
    • A shift away from reliance on U.S. studio licensing
  4. Movies and TV shows diverged in age trends after 2016
    • TV shows got younger every year due to Originals.
    • Movies got older, reflecting:
    • shrinking access to new studio films
    • increased licensing of international back catalogs
    • slower development cycles for original films
  5. Netflix’s international expansion reshaped its content mix
    • A massive surge in Indian content after 2016
      • India was the breakout market due to its huge population and robust domestic film industry.
    • Strong growth from the UK
    • A complete absence of China due to regulatory barriers
  6. Netflix’s catalog skews toward mature audiences When ratings are grouped into children / young adult / adult categories, the majority of titles fall into adult or young adult segments. Netflix’s content strategy is clearly oriented toward mature viewers rather than children’s programming.
  7. Content growth correlates with major movements in Netflix’s stock price Overlaying stock price with content additions shows:
    • A strong upward trend in stock price during the 2015–2019 content boom
    • A plateau and decline as content growth slowed in 2020–2021
    • A clear relationship between catalog expansion, international rollout, and market confidence This suggests that investors responded strongly to Netflix’s rapid scaling of its global library.

Limitations & Future Work¶

Stock price is not a perfect metric for success or failure, but a decent indicator. Other metrics that could be used to track success if they were available would be Net Revenue Growth or Subscriber Counts. As stated earlier, this analysis will never be fully complete without seeing data on when titles were removed from the offering. This would give us a better picture of how Netflix is shaping their catalog. The insights from that data could give us a clue as to what countries are growing and which countries are being pruned from the offering.
